content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 12, 2007

Shant Apekian
PAC Director
California Apartment Association
980 Ninth Street, Suite 200
Sacramento, California 95814-2741

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-07-080

Dear Ms. Apekian:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the contribution limitation provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

If an organization has a political action committee, a small contributor committee, and an independent expenditure committee does each committee retain its own contribution limit, or is a single source contributor who wishes to contribute to more than one of the organization's committees restricted to contributing the maximum amount allowed to the political action committee and sharing that limit between the three committees?

CONCLUSION

A single source contributor does not have to share the contribution limits between committees because contribution limits are specific to each committee regardless of whether or not the committees are controlled by the same organization.

FACTS

You are the Political Action Committee Director for the California Apartment Association (CAA). CAA currently has three sponsored political action committees: State Political Action Committee (SPAC), Small Contributor Committee (SCC), and Independent

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-07-080
Page No. 2


Expenditure Committee (IEC). You would like to know if a single source would have to share
the limits between each committee or are the limits independent of each other.

ANALYSIS

Section 85303 provides that a person may not make a contribution of more than $5,000 to
any committee other than a political party committee when the contribution is made for the
purpose of making a non-political party contribution to a candidate for elective state office. This limit
has subsequently been raised to $6,000.² Generally, when a contribution is made to a non-political
party committee for a purpose other than to make a contribution to a candidate's candidacy for
elective state office the $6,000 contribution limit does not apply.³ There are no limits on
contributions made to recipient committees that only make independent expenditures. (Section
85303(c).) In the special case of the small contributor committee, campaign funds may not
include any contributions that exceed $200 per person per calendar year (Section 85203;
Regulation 18503(a)(3)).

The CAA has set up three different types of committees and each committee is subject to
its own limitations, independent of the other two committees. Therefore, the SPAC may receive
contributions of up to $6,000 per year from a single source, in turn the SCC may receive a
contribution of up to $200 in a calendar year from that same source and the IEC may receive
unlimited contributions from that same source.⁴

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl
I:\AdviceLtrs\07-080


² Regulation 18545(a)(7) provides that the adjusted annual contribution limit to any committee, other than a
political party committee is now $6,000 per person.
³ Section 85310 places restrictions on contributions made for producing communications identifying state
candidates.
⁴ Though you have only sought advice on contribution limits when CAA's committees receive
contributions, assuming CAA's committees are directed and controlled by the same person or a majority of the same
persons, there are aggregation requirements when CAA's committees make contributions. (See Section 85311, copy
enclosed.)
